1: The healing of the centurion's servant (7:1-10) appears in both Luke and Matthew (8:5-13), with Luke adding details that reflect a Gentile patron-client relationship: the centurion sends Jewish elders first (respected intermediaries) and then friends (personal representatives), never approaching Jesus directly. This distance-protocol reflects Mediterranean honor-shame culture: the centurion recognizes that direct approach would obligate Jesus and risk social boundary-violation. His own statement (I am not worthy for you to come under my roof) is a precise formulation of the patron-client inequality. Jesus's wonder at his faith (v.9) — the only time in the Synoptics Jesus marvels at faith — singles out the Gentile as the paradigm believer.
2: A Roman centurion commanded a centuria of approximately 80 soldiers; the centurions were the professional backbone of the Roman army, often men of 15-20 years’ service. Capernaum was a garrison town on the Via Maris (the main commercial route from Egypt to Damascus); the presence of Roman soldiers in the Galilean fishing town was part of the occupation infrastructure. The centurion’s concern for a sick slave exceeds economic logic; the word entimos (highly valued, honored) indicates an unusual personal attachment or exceptional usefulness.
3: The centurion sends Jewish presbyteroi (elders, respected community leaders) as his intermediaries — a culturally sophisticated move in the patron-client Mediterranean world. By using community leaders as advocates, he acknowledges the social hierarchy: he is a Gentile of Roman authority in a Jewish town; direct approach to a Jewish teacher would risk the boundary violation that his subsequent message (v.6) will explicitly acknowledge. The Jewish elders’ advocacy shows that the centurion has cultivated genuine relationships in the Jewish community.
4: “He deserves to have you do this for him” (axios estin hō parechē touto) — the argument is made in honor-shame terms: the centurion’s benefactions toward the Jewish community create a reciprocal obligation. This is the patronage-system logic: gifts create bonds of charis (gratitude and obligation); one who has given much can legitimately expect response. The elders are invoking this system on the centurion’s behalf — the unusual spectacle of Jewish community leaders interceding for a Roman officer.
5: “He loves our nation and has built our synagogue” — the phenomenon of Gentile synagogue-patrons is well-attested epigraphically. The Theodotus inscription (Jerusalem, pre-70 CE) mentions a synagogue built for pilgrims; inscriptions from the Aphrodisias synagogue (ca. 3rd century CE) list “God-fearers” (theosebeis) as donors alongside full Jewish members. Such patrons of the Jewish community were called “God-fearers” (sebomenoi ton theon or phoboumenoi ton theon) — Gentiles who attended synagogue and observed some Jewish practices without full conversion.
6: “Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof” — the centurion’s message through friends reflects his awareness of the Jewish purity concern: entering a Gentile home could render a Jew ritually impure (m. Ohalot 18:7 discusses Gentile dwellings as potentially impure); a pious Jew would hesitate to enter. The centurion’s sensitivity to this boundary — unusual for a Roman officer — is part of what makes his faith extraordinary from Jesus’s perspective.
7: “But say the word, and my servant will be healed” (eipē logō) — the centurion’s conceptual model is the Roman chain of command: authority operates through words that are effective independent of the speaker’s physical presence. The word of a centurion, backed by imperial authority, is obeyed without his physical supervision. He reasons analogically: if Roman command-authority works this way, then Jesus’s command-authority (backed by divine authority) should be no different. This military analogy will be the explanation Jesus offers for why this is “great faith.”
8: “I too am a man set under authority” — the centurion’s self-description positions him within a vertical authority structure (emperor → legate → tribune → centurion). His claim is symmetric: just as I exercise authority derived from a higher authority, so you exercise authority derived from the one whose servant I see you as. The military model of delegated authority becomes the framework for understanding divine healing: Jesus speaks with the authority of the one who sent him, and disease must obey as soldiers obey a centurion’s order.
9: “Not even in Israel have I found such faith” — the comparison to Israel is explicit and unprecedented. In the Gospels, Jesus marvels (ethaumasen) only twice: here at the centurion’s faith, and in Mark 6:6 at Israel’s unbelief. The Gentile’s faith stands as an implicit rebuke of the covenant people’s unresponsiveness. The pattern anticipates Luke’s major theme of Gentile inclusion and Jewish-leadership rejection, worked out in full in Acts: the mission moves to the Gentiles precisely because Israel’s leaders reject the message.
10: “They found the servant well” (hygiainonta ton doulon) — the healing at a distance is confirmed by the returned messengers who find the servant healthy. Luke’s understatement here is telling: no second meeting with the centurion, no extended description of the healing, simply the verification that what Jesus’s implicit word accomplished is now visible fact. The narrative closure mirrors the centurion’s own faith-structure: he trusted in the authority of the word before any verification; the verification comes as confirmation, not as the basis of faith.
11: Nain (modern Nein) is a village in the lower Galilee, in the Valley of Jezreel about 6 miles southeast of Nazareth and 25 miles southwest of Capernaum. The proximity to Shunem (about 5 miles away) where Elisha raised the Shunammite’s son (2 Kgs 4:18-37) would not have been lost on a Jewish audience. Luke’s immediate juxtaposition of the centurion miracle (faith of a Gentile) and the Nain widow miracle (compassion for a Jewish widow) maintains his dual-audience sensitivity.
12: The town gate (pylē tēs poleōs) was the standard location of community life in ancient Palestinian towns: legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and social gathering all occurred at the gate (Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:10-12). A funeral procession leaving the town (the dead were buried outside the settlement boundaries) would pass through this public space. The widow’s situation is identified precisely: monogenēs (only son) and chēra (widow) — double vulnerability; in the patriarchal economy, she has lost both her husband’s and now her son’s economic support.
13: “When the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her” (esplanchnisthē ep autē) — the verb splagchnizomai (to be moved in the gut, to have deep compassion) is used in Luke for Jesus (7:13), the Good Samaritan (10:33), and the father of the prodigal son (15:20). In the LXX it translates the Hebrew racham (womb-compassion, the compassion a mother has for her child). The narrator identifies Jesus as “the Lord” (ho Kyrios) here — a post-resurrection title used in Luke’s narrative voice, signaling the divine character of the compassion.
14: “Touched the bier” (hepsato tēs sorou) — a deliberate ritual-impurity act: Numbers 19:11,16 specifies that contact with a corpse or anything the corpse touched rendered one impure for seven days; the bier carrying a corpse would transmit impurity. Jesus touches the bier publicly, before the crowd. The action is a sign of sovereignty over the impurity system: rather than the impurity spreading to Jesus, the healing power flows from Jesus to the corpse. The pattern repeats in the leper-touching (5:13) and the hemorrhaging woman (8:43-48).
15: “He gave him back to his mother” (edōken auton tē mētri autou) — verbatim parallel to 1 Kgs 17:23 LXX: “And Elijah gave him to his mother” after raising the widow of Zarephath’s son. Luke’s language is not accidental — it is a deliberate echo that positions Jesus as a prophet-like-Elijah (Deut 18:15-18: the prophet like Moses), while simultaneously exceeding Elijah: Elijah labored over the child repeatedly; Jesus speaks a single command. The crowd’s recognition (v.16: “A great prophet has appeared”) confirms the intended allusion.
16: “God has visited his people” (epeskepsato ho theos ton laon autou) — the divine visitation (episkopē) is a major Lukan theme: Zechariah’s Benedictus uses it twice (1:68: “he has visited and redeemed”; 1:78: “the dawn from on high will visit us”). The Hebrew paqad (to visit, attend to, inspect) describes God’s saving intervention in Exodus (3:16: “I have surely visited you”). The crowd’s theological instinct is sound: what they are seeing is a divine visitation, the God of Israel coming to help his people through the prophet Jesus.
17: “This news spread throughout Judea and the surrounding region” — the geographic spread (from Galilee to Judea and beyond) follows the pattern of prophetic fame in the OT: the fame of Elijah and Elisha spread throughout Israel and into Syria (2 Kgs 5:1-8). In Luke-Acts, the geographic spread of the message is theologically significant: it anticipates Acts 1:8 (Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, ends of the earth). The Nain miracle is the first major event that pushes Jesus’s fame beyond Galilee’s regional boundaries.
18: John’s disciples in the prison (Herod Antipas imprisoned John in the fortress of Machaerus east of the Dead Sea, according to Josephus, Ant. 18.119) communicate news to John through a network of followers who visited prisoners. Prison visits were permitted in the ancient world and were essential for prisoner survival (families and disciples brought food and supplies). John’s question from prison reflects either genuine uncertainty about Jesus’s messianic identity, a desire to have his disciples confirm it directly, or a pastoral pedagogy for his disciples’ benefit.
19: “Ho erchomenos” (the coming one) was a recognized messianic title from Ps 118:26 (“blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord”), used also at the Triumphal Entry (19:38). 4Q521 (a Qumran fragment sometimes called the Messianic Apocalypse) describes the messianic age bringing healing to the blind, the deaf, and the poor — the precise categories Jesus will enumerate in v.22. John’s question presupposes a specific messianic profile; Jesus’s answer will confirm some elements while reconfiguring others.
20: The two disciples as legal witnesses (Deut 19:15: a matter must be established by two or three witnesses) ensures that John’s confirmation of Jesus’s answer carries legal weight: two eyewitnesses can testify to what they heard and saw. The use of the legal-witness structure for a theological inquiry is typical of Luke’s concern for verifiable testimony (asphaleia, certainty, is promised to Theophilus in 1:4). John wants a fact-based confirmation, not a secondhand report.
21: “At that very time Jesus cured many” — the response to John’s question is performative and demonstrative rather than propositional: Jesus answers by doing in front of the messengers. The enumeration of healings (diseases, sicknesses, evil spirits, blindness) provides the evidence base for the reply of v.22. This is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus performs healings specifically for the benefit of witnesses who will report them; the healing becomes testimony.
22: The catalogue of messianic signs quotes Isa 35:5-6 (blind see, deaf hear, lame leap, mute sing) and Isa 61:1 (good news for the poor). 4Q521’s list of messianic-age events includes: “he will heal the wounded, give life to the dead, and preach good news to the poor” — a near-exact match to Jesus’s reply. The raising of the dead (not in Isa 35 or 61 but in Isa 26:19) is added; it escalates the list to maximum. John is to understand that the messianic age has arrived even though it does not match every feature of his announced fire-and-judgment vision (3:16-17).
23: “Blessed is the one who does not stumble on account of me” (skandalisthē en emoi) — skandalon (stumbling block) was a trap-trigger that springs a snare; to be scandalised by Jesus is to be caught and immobilized by his unexpected form. The beatitude is also a gentle rebuke: John had announced a “coming one” who would baptize with fire and clear the threshing floor (3:16-17); Jesus is baptizing with the Spirit and healing the sick, not judging the wicked. The beatitude asks John to expand his messianic expectations.
24: “What did you go out into the wilderness to see?” — the three-question rhetorical structure (reed in the wind, fine clothes, prophet?) invites the crowd to reconsider what they already know about John. The wilderness (erēmos) setting is theologically charged: it is the place of divine testing and preparation (Israel’s 40 years, Elijah’s 40-day journey, Jesus’s 40 days). A reed shaken by the wind (kalamon hypo anemou saleuomenon) is someone who bends to every social pressure — the opposite of John, who stood against Herod and the Pharisees.
25: “A man dressed in fine clothes?” — the contrast with John’s camel-hair clothing and leather belt (implied by the Elijah identification, 2 Kgs 1:8) is sharp. “Fine clothes and luxury” (en himatismō endoxō kai tryphe) describe Herod Antipas’s court at Tiberias — John is emphatically not in the royal court but in the royal prison, precisely because he refused to accommodate royal pressure. The question implies: those who bend toward luxury and fine clothes are in palaces, not in prison.
26: “More than a prophet” (perissoteron prophētou) — John exceeds the OT prophets not in personal holiness but in salvation-historical position: the prophets predicted a future they did not see; John stands at the threshold, personally introducing the one they predicted. The same logic appears in Hebrews 11:39-40 (the OT saints did not receive what was promised; they were waiting for “us”). John’s unique position (last of the old order, herald of the new) makes him greater in prophetic role while lesser in covenant position than the least disciple who stands fully in the new age.
27: The Malachi 3:1 citation (“I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you”) is quoted in modified form: the original has God speaking in first person about his own coming; Luke’s version reattributes it as God speaking to Jesus (“before you”). This subtle shift is a high Christological claim embedded in a citation: what was said about God’s forerunner is now applied to Jesus’s forerunner. Malachi 4:5 identified this forerunner as Elijah — hence the later tradition identifying John as Elijah-redivivus (Matt 11:14; 17:12).
28: “Among those born of women there is no one greater than John” — the Semitic idiom “born of women” (gennētos gynaikōn) means a mortal human being (cf. Job 14:1: “Mortals, born of woman, live but a few days”); the phrase places John at the pinnacle of human history as God’s last and greatest herald. “Yet the least in the kingdom is greater than he” — the kingdom introduces a new order of privilege: even the newest member of the kingdom stands on the far side of the watershed event John could only announce, thereby having received more than John possessed.
29: “All the people, even the tax collectors, when they heard Jesus’s words, acknowledged that God’s way was right” (edikaiosān ton theon, they justified/declared-righteous God) — by receiving John’s baptism, the people publicly acknowledged the validity of his prophetic call and thus vindicated God’s sending of John. The pairing of “all the people” and “even the tax collectors” creates the broad populist response to set against the elite rejection: tax collectors (telōnai) were generally despised as Roman collaborators and social outcasts, yet they responded where the religious leaders did not.
30: “The Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves” (tēn boulēn tou theou ēthetēsan) — boulē (plan, counsel, purpose) is a major Lukan concept for the divine plan that encompasses the entire salvation history: Acts 2:23 (Jesus delivered up “by God’s deliberate plan and foreknowledge”); Acts 4:28 (the passion fulfills what God’s hand and plan had decided). Rejecting John’s baptism was not merely a personal religious choice but a refusal of participation in the divine salvation-plan — with consequences that unfold throughout Luke-Acts.
31: “To what shall I compare the people of this generation?” — the parable of the children in the market-place (agora) addresses the inconsistency of the religious leaders who rejected both John and Jesus on contradictory grounds. The “generation” (genea) is Luke’s consistent term for the resistant religious leadership (cf. 9:41; 11:29-32,50-51; 17:25; 21:32); it does not mean everyone alive at the time but the contemporaneous leadership who reject the prophets.
32: “We played the pipe for you and you did not dance / we sang a dirge and you did not weep” — children’s playing of wedding and funeral games was a common sight in the agora (marketplace, the social center of town life). The parable describes the perverse inconsistency of the generation: it refused to celebrate when celebration was called for (John’s fast = mourning-game refused) and refused to mourn when mourning was called for (Jesus’s feast = wedding-game refused). No response to God’s messengers is deemed appropriate; the problem is not the form of the message but the heart of the hearers.
33: “John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine” — John’s abstinence from bread and wine may reflect a Nazirite vow (1:15: no wine or strong drink; Num 6:3-4 prohibits the Nazirite from wine and all grape products). The accusation of demon-possession (daimonion echei) was the default charge against those whose behavior was inexplicable by normal social categories; the Beelzebul controversy (11:14-22) will repeat the pattern with Jesus himself. Abnormal behavior = supernatural causation; the question is which supernatural agent.
34: “The Son of Man came eating and drinking” — the contrast between John’s asceticism and Jesus’s table-fellowship is historical and theological: Jesus ate with sinners, tax collectors, Pharisees, and crowds, making table-fellowship a primary medium of the kingdom. The epithet thrown at him (“glutton and drunkard” = phagos kai oinopotēs) echoes Deut 21:20’s description of the rebellious son: “This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious; he is a glutton and a drunkard.” The Deuteronomic rebellious son was stoned; Jesus is accused of the same pattern.
35: “Wisdom is proved right by all her children” — the personification of divine Wisdom (Sophia) as a figure who sends her agents (Prov 9:3; Sir 24; Wis 7:24-27) is the implicit framework: John and Jesus are Wisdom’s messengers or children, and their vindication comes not from the generation’s acceptance but from the fruit of their lives. Q 7:35 has “her children” where Matthew 11:19 has “her deeds”; Luke’s version identifies John and Jesus as Wisdom’s children — they prove Wisdom right by their ministries.
36: “One of the Pharisees invited Jesus to have dinner with him” (katelithe) — reclining at dinner (kataklinō) was the posture of the Greco-Roman formal meal: diners reclined on low couches arranged around a central table. Feet extended away from the table, which is why the woman stands behind Jesus at his feet (v.38). Pharisee dinner invitations to Jesus appear three times in Luke (7:36; 11:37; 14:1) — the relationship between Jesus and the Pharisees is not simple mutual hostility but a complex mixture of interest, critique, and occasional fellowship.
37: “A woman in that town who had lived a sinful life” (hamartōlos) — in the context of a formal dinner, the identification as a known sinner (likely a prostitute, the most common reference for women publicly identified as sinners in this period) makes her presence at the dinner a severe social transgression. Formal dinner parties in antiquity were gender-segregated for respectable women; a prostitute’s appearance would have been scandalous to the assembled guests. Her bringing an alabaster jar of myron (expensive perfume/ointment) suggests some degree of financial means.
38: “Began to wet his feet with her tears, and wiped them with her hair” — the sequence of actions (tears, hair-wiping, kissing feet, perfume) escalates from spontaneous grief to deliberate honor. Hair-letting in public was generally associated with mourning or disreputable women; anointing feet rather than the head was a reversal of normal honor (hosts anointed guests’ heads, not feet). The woman’s acts are excessive, counter-cultural, and deeply personal; they constitute a sustained act of devotion that social convention makes scandalous.
39: “If this man were a prophet, he would know who is touching him” — Simon’s internal reasoning assumes that prophetic knowledge includes discernment of ritual status. The prophet’s ability to perceive hidden realities (cf. 2 Kgs 5:25-27, Elisha knowing Gehazi’s secret) would, in Simon’s framework, include knowing the woman’s sinful status and therefore withdrawing from her touch. Jesus’s apparent unawareness seems to Simon like prophetic failure — evidence that he is not the prophet he is claimed to be.
40: Jesus’s address to Simon (“I have something to tell you”) takes the form of the parable-introduction: the teacher invites the student into a thought experiment rather than issuing a direct rebuke. Simon’s response (Didaskale, Teacher) is respectful; the social dynamic is host-honored-guest, not hostile opponent. Jesus’s parabolic method here — as throughout Luke — is to lead the hearer into self-conviction through a story they can evaluate neutrally before its application lands on them.
41: “A certain moneylender” (danistēs) — the lending industry occupied a complex legal space in Jewish society. Torah prohibited interest between Jews (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19-20) but permitted it with Gentiles. The Hillel-era prosbul was a legal instrument allowing loans near the Sabbatical year without the automatic debt-release; it effectively enabled a lending economy. Five hundred denarii (approximately two years’ wages for a day laborer) vs. fifty denarii (approximately two months’ wages) creates a 10:1 ratio for the debt comparison.
42: “He forgave the debts of both” (echarisato amphoterois) — the verb charizomai (to graciously give, to forgive) is used for both commercial debt-cancellation and divine forgiveness of sin; the word play drives the parable’s logic. The Jubilee principle of Deut 15:1-11 (release of debts every seventh year) and Lev 25 (the full Jubilee every 49/50 years) is the biblical background for understanding debt-forgiveness as a theological concept: God’s forgiveness is the ultimate Jubilee cancellation of debt.
43: Simon’s “I suppose” (hypolambanō) indicates reluctant assent; the logic is inescapable but the conclusion will be uncomfortable. Jesus confirms the judgment (“you have judged correctly”) — using the very act of judging correctly about the parable to position Simon as the judge of his own behavior toward the woman. The parable-as-trap structure is typical of Nathan’s parable to David (2 Sam 12:1-7): the story draws a judgment from the listener that then applies to themselves.
44: “Do you see this woman?” (blepeis tautēn tēn gynaika) — the three contrast-pairs (water for feet / tears; greeting kiss / continuous foot-kissing; oil for head / perfume for feet) enumerate the standard hospitality courtesies of the period. Providing water for a guest’s feet-washing was the minimum hospitality (Gen 18:4; 1 Sam 25:41); a kiss of greeting (philēma) was the standard welcome; anointing the head with oil (Ps 23:5) was a gesture of honor. Simon provided none of these; the woman provided more than all of them.
45: “You did not give me a kiss” — the hospitality kiss (philēma) was the standard greeting between men of equal or near-equal social status in the Mediterranean world. Simon’s failure to greet Jesus with a kiss may indicate that he regarded Jesus as socially inferior (a Galilean craftsman/teacher without official credentials) despite the dinner invitation. Or it may be simple negligence. Either way, his omission is contrasted with the woman’s ou dieleipen kataphilousa (she has not stopped kissing — continuous, intense kissing of the feet).
46: “You did not put oil on my head, but she poured perfume on my feet” — the inversion of expected locations (head vs. feet) underscores the woman’s excessive reversal of honor-protocol: she honors the lowest part with the most expensive substance. Elaion (olive oil, used for normal anointing) vs. myron (costly perfume/ointment imported from the East) — the woman’s choice of myron and its application to the feet is a conspicuously inverted act of extravagant devotion, comparable to Mary of Bethany’s anointing (John 12:3).
47: “Her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown” — the cause-effect direction is clarified by the parable: forgiveness precedes and produces love; love is the evidence of forgiveness already given, not the merit that earns it. The Greek construction (hoti ēgapēsen poly = because she loved much) can mean either “she is forgiven because she loved much” (love = basis) or “how much she loved is the proof that she has been forgiven much” (love = evidence). The parable of vv.41-42 settles the interpretation: the greater the forgiven debt, the greater the resulting love.
48: “Your sins are forgiven” (aphiontai sou hai hamartiai) — the present passive indicative: they stand forgiven, the forgiveness is in effect. The public pronouncement in the Pharisee’s house parallels the private pronouncement to the paralytic (5:20: aphiontai soi hai hamartiai sou). In both cases the announcement is public and the controversy focuses on who has the authority to pronounce it. The synagogue/dining room is the social venue of maximum impact: the forgiveness is declared where shame was publicly attached.
49: “Who is this who even forgives sins?” (tis houtos estin hos kai hamartias aphiēsin) — the guests’ question echoes the scribes’ internal questioning at 5:21 (“Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone?”). The Christological question is raised explicitly but left unanswered — Luke’s narrative technique of embedding the right question for the reader to supply. Only God forgives sins; Jesus forgives sins; therefore — the reader must draw the conclusion the text withholds.
50: “Your faith has saved you; go in peace” (hē pistis sou sesōken se) — the formula of dismissal (poreuou eis eirēnēn) echoes Eli’s dismissal of Hannah (1 Sam 1:17: “Go in peace”) and the standard Hebrew blessing. Sesōken (has saved you, perfect tense: the salvation stands complete) encompasses forgiveness, restoration, and social rehabilitation — the comprehensive shalom of the Jubilee year. The woman departs as a complete person, not merely as a forgiven sinner: she has received the full liberation that Jesus announced in Isa 61:1 (4:18).
